author: @garrydysart6709
text: I would like to make 12inch square heat panels. That would be ceiling mounted for my reptiles. I need the panels to emit about 140F. So it emits a heat on the floor 90F. how would u get one of these panels to give off 140F. I will be using dimming thermostats. Add more cooper tape ?

author: @QUADBOYification
text: Simplifying things is a big part of your demonstration. At 45 degrees radiation at that size runs you 40 volts at 2 Amps = 80 Watts. I wont disagree it is a genius process but a gimmick for practical use. It is safe at 40 volts, again i agree. To have a good return on heat and efficiency you at least need 1 m x 1 m PTC floor heating that can run on standard 240 volts. It wil have 500 Watts output and that even is not enough to heat a small space. You really need to pump up the Voltage to 500 Volts and have it produce 1000 Watts of heat. This is ofcourse out of the range of safety and as always your own risk. At the 1000 Watt level you are better of with a convection heater, simple and agian safe.

author: @MrTalhakamran2006
text:
Salam Robert Murray-Smith 
Awww! I just paid 168 CNY for something similar. I am in Shanghai and here it is being sold with the name "Carbon crystal heating". I bought 2 panels of 500 Watts each with a thermostat. After watching this video I am 100% sure its exactly what you have made.

author: @lukelordoflight
text:
Hello Robert!
Thank you so much for sharing this absolutely cool tech-tip with us!

May I ask, if you could somehow get a Infrared camera, to film the heatup process of your Panel-heater, as this would be very important, to see, if the coating and the heat distribution is the same all over the painted area....
Laser -thermometer is nice, but to see the full area, in comparison to the surrounding area, that is interesting and important.

best and kind regards

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good point - i'll try and get one

replies.author: @lukelordoflight
replies.text:
Please let me know your results, when you have them....as I want to cooperate with you and add your conductive Ink to my collection and offer it here in my contry, as I don't wanna spend any more on regular heating system... please PM me, if you can..

THANKS with best and kind regards

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
lukelordoflight
what's your e-mail happy to pm you

author: @pixelspring
text:
Dear Robert,  You truly are a scientist in the old Classical tradition of information sharing.  In those days, even during times of war those good men would transfer information and share to expand knowledge and understanding.  Bringing your chemistry know how out of the labs and into the reach of the common man,  especially with your natural enthusiasm is of truly great service.  Your posts here are totally inspiring.  Your good natured spirit and generosity are abundant and i salute you sir. 
I was wondering if there is a forum where like minds can discuss ideas and swap information with interested parties in what you have invented?   so many interesting possibilities here - it would be great to have a place to start threads and get an online community of experimenters and technicians going!  - from that who knows what great things can grow?     I also want to buy some of your ink to experiment with,.. but i live on the other side of the planet - in the last colony :).- new zealand.

author: @casualjoe2
text: A while ago I made some heated wristbands (anklebands) using heater wire between two pieces of heat conducting tape, which was great for the cold winters we've had. What I found however, was that the heater wire had a 'memory' which made getting it on and off a bit cumbersome.  After seeing this paint I thought it would be great on a flexible plastic wrist (ankle) band, weathers a bit hot for it now mind haha

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Lol - indeed - but this wouldn't be so good for that - well not as good as one of the inks i am working on which is a flexible version of this for exactly what you are proposing

author: @Kroska1
text:
For painting it on a wall, if you have a 16 gauge wire going from the 'heater' leads to a switch and then to a 10 amp breaker I wonder if that would keep the wall (drywall paper) from setting on fire.  With the breaker and grounding it should keep current at or below 10 amps. 

I'll have to buy some of that ink now haha.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: just make sure the temp doesn't go over 60 degrees - a k type thermocouple and pid will do it - you can buy them as a kit for underfloor heating - which is what this is - just in a tin

author: @jeriztattoo
text: How flexible is the paint? Can i paint it on fabric or silicon rubber or will it crack? I was thinking of trying to make heating elements to fit in shoes, gloves or a jacket that you could just plug into the power of a snowmobile or a motorcycle

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's silicate based so has low flexibility - it'll cope with the flex most buildings experience but it wouldn't work so well on cloth - i am however working on a cloth version
